food_subgroup: Seaweed and algae dishes — cold dressed

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general kelp food product class; no specific entry for the dressed Chinese cold salad preparation exists. Closest defensible anchor used.
ontology.foodex2_description: Seaweeds and other algae — prepared / processed
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A00LC covers processed seaweed products. No sub-code specific to cold-dressed shredded kelp salad exists; closest parent match used and documented.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT clinical food concept found for shredded kelp salad as a prepared dish.

preparation_method: dressing with soy sauce, vinegar, sesame oil, chili oil, garlic

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical single serving of Shredded Kelp Salad as a cold appetizer or side dish is approximately 100 g, equivalent to a small bowl.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Seaweed, kelp, raw (FDC ID 168457) and composite estimation for dressed preparation
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Raw kelp nutrient profile from USDA FoodData Central (FDC ID 168457) used as the primary anchor. Macronutrient values adjusted upward for the addition of sesame oil (~3 g fat contribution per 100 g serving) and sodium from soy sauce (~420 mg/100 g). Micronutrient values for calcium, magnesium, iron, potassium, and vitamins are consistent with published kelp composition data. Folate value of 180 mcg/100 g reflects the composite dish including garlic and coriander; retained from input with medium confidence pending direct dish-level analysis. Vitamin B12 of 0.5 mcg/100 g is plausible for kelp-based preparations per peer-reviewed seaweed nutrition literature.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — seaweed / algae analogous estimate; Atkinson FS et al., Diabetes Care 2021
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for shredded kelp salad as a prepared dish. GI estimated at 17 based on the closest analogous food: seaweed / algae preparations with high soluble fibre content (alginates, fucoidans) from the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2021 international GI tables. The presence of Chinkiang vinegar and sesame oil further attenuates postprandial glucose response. Glycemic load calculated as (GI × available carbohydrate per serving) / 100 = (17 × 6.2 × 1.0) / 100 ≈ 1.1 per 100 g serving. Confidence: medium.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content from soy sauce (~420 mg/100 g)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size or request reduced-sodium soy sauce, as a 100 g serving contributes approximately 18% of the 2300 mg daily sodium limit recommended by major cardiovascular guidelines.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low glycemic index (~17) and low glycemic load (~1.1 per serving) due to high soluble fibre (alginates) in kelp
type2_diabetes.guidance: Shredded kelp salad is well-suited for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes; its high soluble fibre content slows gastric emptying and blunts postprandial glucose excursions, and the vinegar dressing provides an additional glucose-attenuating effect.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Cholesterol-free, rich in potassium, magnesium, and omega-3 precursor fatty acids from kelp; moderate sodium warrants attention
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Kelp salad provides cardioprotective minerals (potassium 120 mg, magnesium 110 mg per 100 g) and is cholesterol-free, but the sodium from soy sauce should be monitored in patients on sodium-restricted cardiac diets.

thyroid_iodine_sensitivity.key_factor: Kelp is one of the richest dietary sources of iodine; excessive intake may disrupt thyroid function
thyroid_iodine_sensitivity.guidance: Individuals with thyroid disorders or those on thyroid medication should consult their physician before consuming kelp regularly, as iodine content can be highly variable and potentially excessive.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Shredded Kelp Salad (凉拌海带丝, Liángbàn Hǎidàisī) is a classic Chinese cold appetizer with deep roots in Northern Chinese and Sichuan culinary traditions, where it is served as a refreshing starter before the main meal. The dish begins with dried or fresh kelp (kombu, Laminaria japonica) that is rehydrated if necessary, then cut into thin julienne strips and briefly blanched in boiling water for one to two minutes to achieve a tender-crisp texture while removing any raw bitterness. The blanched kelp is immediately refreshed in cold water to halt cooking and preserve its characteristic firm, slightly chewy bite. The cooled strips are then hand-tossed in a boldly flavoured dressing assembled from light soy sauce for umami depth, Chinkiang black vinegar (镇江香醋) for tangy acidity, freshly minced garlic for pungency, toasted sesame oil for nutty richness, and chili oil (红油) for a gentle Sichuan heat. The finished salad is garnished with toasted sesame seeds and, optionally, fresh coriander (cilantro). The sensory profile is a harmonious balance of savoury, sour, nutty, and mildly spicy notes layered over the oceanic, mineral-forward flavour of kelp, with a satisfying crunch that distinguishes it from softer seaweed preparations. Nutritionally, a 100 g serving delivers approximately 55 kcal, 1.8 g protein, 3.5 g fat (primarily unsaturated, from sesame and chili oils), and 6.2 g carbohydrate with 2.5 g dietary fibre. It is an excellent source of calcium (150 mg), magnesium (110 mg), and iron (2.5 mg), and is naturally cholesterol-free and vegan. The glycemic index is very low (estimated ~17), making it highly suitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes or metabolic syndrome, as the soluble fibres in kelp — alginates and fucoidans — slow gastric emptying and blunt postprandial glucose excursions; the vinegar dressing provides an additional glucose-attenuating effect. Sodium content (~420 mg/100 g from soy sauce) warrants moderation for individuals with hypertension or on sodium-restricted cardiac diets. Kelp is one of the richest dietary sources of iodine, which supports thyroid hormone synthesis, but individuals with thyroid disorders should seek medical guidance before regular consumption. The dish is a staple starter at Chinese restaurant meals, a popular home-cooked snack, and is widely consumed across East Asia as a mineral-rich, low-calorie accompaniment to rice-based meals.

confidence_reason: Core macronutrients are anchored to USDA FoodData Central raw kelp data (FDC ID 168457) with documented adjustments for the dressed preparation. Folate (180 mcg/100 g) and vitamin B12 (0.5 mcg/100 g) are elevated relative to raw kelp alone and are retained from the input record with medium confidence pending dish-level laboratory analysis. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database framework. No exact dish-level entry exists in any approved primary source for this specific preparation.
notes: 1. water_g set to -1: moisture content for the dressed kelp salad preparation is not reported in USDA FoodData Central or any other approved source consulted as of 2026-04-17. 2. Folate value of 180 mcg/100 g is higher than typical raw kelp values; plausible for the composite dish including garlic and coriander garnish but should be verified by laboratory analysis. 3. Vitamin B12 of 0.5 mcg/100 g is consistent with published seaweed B12 data but bioavailability of seaweed B12 analogues is debated in the literature. 4. Saturated fat (0.5 g), monounsaturated fat (1.2 g), and polyunsaturated fat (1.4 g) are estimated from the sesame oil and chili oil components using USDA fatty acid profiles; trans_fat_g confirmed 0.0 for this preparation. 5. Iodine content is not listed in per_100g as it is not a standard RESULT template field, but kelp is one of the richest dietary iodine sources and this is flagged in health_context. 6. Duplicate portion entry in input (two identical 100 g entries) was collapsed to a single 100 g serving. 7. This is a Chinese dish, not a Singaporean dish; Malay, Tamil, and Hindi local names are not applicable and are set to null.
